functions.name: azureml.studio.internal.io.rwbuffer_manager.pipeline_io
documentation: Write some data with write_func, then redirect the data to read_func.

classes.name: azureml.studio.internal.io.rwbuffer_manager.RWBufferManager
documentation:
A buffer manager that can write data first, then read the written data.

```pycon
>>> mng = RWBufferManager()
>>> data = 'some string'
>>> with mng.open('w') as fout:
...     fout.write(data)
11
>>> with mng.open('r') as fin:
...     fin.read()
'some string'
```

classes.name: azureml.studio.internal.io.rwbuffer_manager.AzureMLOutput

methods.name: azureml.studio.internal.io.rwbuffer_manager.AzureMLOutput.open
documentation:
Return an AzureMLOutput object for writing data and uploading to azureml.

```
param run

an azureml Run object for uploading file.

param path

the path to upload in azureml.

param write_mode

the write mode(w/wb) for writing data.

return

```
An interface that can upload data to azureml.

documentation:
```pycon
>>> is_null_or_empty("")
True
>>> is_null_or_empty(None)
True
>>> is_null_or_empty(" ")
False
```

```pycon
>>> is_null_or_whitespace("")

True
>>> is_null_or_whitespace(" ")
True
```

functions.name: azureml.studio.internal.types.parse_timedelta
documentation:
Convert a C# like string representation of TimeSpan to python timedelta.

Valid format:

: [-][d.]H:m\[:s\[.f\[f\[f\[f\[f[f]\]\]\]\]\]\]

Example:

: "1:2", "1:02", "01:02" -> 1h 2m
  "01:02:03" -> 1h 2m 3s
  "-01:02:03" -> -1h 2m 3s
  "4.01:02:03" -> 4d 1h 2m 3s
  "4.01:02:03.46" -> 4d 1h 2m 3s 460ms
  "4.01:02:03.468357" -> 4d 1h 2m 3s 468ms 357μs

```
param s

string to convert.

return

a timedelta value.
```

methods.name: azureml.studio.internal.utils.dependencies.Dependencies.create_default
documentation:
Default conda environment configuration.

Yaml description is as follows:

name: project_environment
channels:
- defaults
dependencies:
- python=3.8.10

methods.name: azureml.studio.internal.utils.dependencies.Dependencies.update_from_default
documentation: Creates a Dependencies object, updating from the one generated by `create_default()`.

methods.name: azureml.studio.internal.utils.dependencies.Dependencies.to_dict
documentation: This is called when extracting module json spec file.

methods.name: azureml.studio.internal.utils.dependencies.Dependencies.conda_dependency_dict
documentation:
Merge raw data lists into a conda environment compatible dict, which will be dumped to a YAML file.

For conda environment file format please refer to:
https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html#creating-an-environment-file-manually

Rules for generating conda environment YAML file:
  1. Firstly, sort each of the lists alphabetically.
  2. Merge PipOptions and PipPackages lists into one list. PipOptions comes before PipPackages.
  3. Create a dictionary object with key 'pip', value is the list created in step 2.
  4. Append this dictionary to the end of CondaPackages. (Only if the list created in step 2 is not empty.)
  5. Create a dictionary with the following keys (make sure to keep the order):
     * 'name': is hard-coded 'project_environment'
     * 'channels': the CondaChannel list. if list is empty, set to a default list as ['defaults'].
     * 'dependencies': is the CondaPackages list with pip appended to the end (if any).
  6. Dump this dict to a yaml file.

WARNING:
     Do NOT change this implementation.

     AzureML service will create a hash for the generated YAML file,
     which will be used as the key of the docker image cache.
     If the implementation changed, and the image cache may fail to match,
     causing performance problems.

WARNING 2:
     There is also a C# version of this logic in the JES code base.

     ref: `ConstructCondaDependency` method in the following code:
     https://msdata.visualstudio.com/AzureML/_git/StudioCore?path=%2FProduct%2FSource%2FStudioCoreService%2FCommon%2FCommonHelper.cs&version=GBmaster

     Please keep the C# code synced in case the implementation here
     must be changed in the future.
